chemicals.concentration_notes: ◦ These crystals are found throughout the entire plant, specifically in the leaves and stems26. ◦ The toxicity is generally classified as moderate6. The immediate pain usually limits the amount a cat will ingest, serving as a deterrent against consuming lethal quantities
chemicals.description: in cats: ◦ Physical Trauma: These crystals are stored in specialized plant cells called idioblasts. When a cat chews or crushes the plant tissue, these cells burst, forcefully ejecting the needle-like crystals into the soft tissues of the mouth, tongue, and throat3. ◦ Immediate Irritation: This mechanism acts like microscopic needles, causing immediate and intense pain, burning sensations, and inf
chemicals.name: Insoluble Calcium Oxalate crystals (often occurring in needle-like bundles called raphides)

chemicals.concentration_notes: ◦ These are present alongside the oxalate crystals in the leaves and stems2.• ,6 Cat toxicity chart for 50 popular houseplants2 • ,4,3,7,5 Houseplants and Ornamentals Toxic to Animals - Toxicology - Merck Veterinary Manual
chemicals.description: in cats: ◦ These toxins are thought to work in conjunction with the calcium oxalate crystals to exacerbate the inflammatory response and irritation in the mouth and gastrointestinal tract
chemicals.name: Proteinaceous toxins (specific enzymes or proteins are not named in the text)

description:
Flamingo Flower and Anthurium (Anthurium spp.) are tropical houseplants with glossy leaves and bright, waxy-looking flowers. They are toxic to cats if chewed. All parts of the plant should be kept away from cats.

The danger comes from insoluble calcium oxalate crystals, sometimes called raphides. These tiny needle-like crystals are released when the plant is bitten. They can pierce and irritate the soft tissues of the mouth and throat, causing immediate pain and swelling.

Signs often start right away and may include pawing at the mouth, drooling, lip or tongue swelling, vomiting, or trouble swallowing. Most cats stop chewing quickly because it hurts, but swelling can still be stressful. Rinse the mouth gently with water if your cat allows it, remove the plant, and contact a veterinarian if symptoms continue or swallowing seems difficult.
name: Flamingo Flower / Anthurium
safetyNotes: Main hazard: Insoluble calcium oxalate crystals cause immediate mouth and throat irritation.
Symptoms: Pawing at the mouth, drooling, swollen lips or tongue, vomiting, or trouble swallowing.
Risk level: All parts are irritating; pain often stops the cat from eating a large amount.
Action: Remove plant material, offer water, and call a vet if swelling or swallowing trouble continues.

symptoms.name: Oral pain, drooling, or pawing at the mouth
Vomiting or trouble swallowing
symptoms.onset: Usually soon after oral irritation begins.

treatments.description: The immediate priority is to gently remove any remaining plant material from the cat's mouth. The mouth is then thoroughly rinsed or wiped out with cool water to flush away the needle-like crystals and inflammatory toxins causing the irritation.
treatments.name: Oral Irrigation / Decontamination
treatments.notes: This step helps stop the immediate burning sensation. Because the pain is caused by microscopic crystals piercing the tissue (rather than a chemical absorption that takes time), rapid physical removal of the irritant is the most effective initial intervention.Administration of Demulcents (Soothing A

treatments.description: The cat may be offered milk, yogurt, or other calcium-containing liquid sources.
treatments.name: Administration of Calcium-Containing Dairy Products
treatments.notes: Veterinary sources note that insoluble oxalates often bind to calcium; dairy products can help bind these crystals and aid in clearing them from the oral cavity and esophagus, providing relief from pain and irritation.Airway Assessment and Monitoring

treatments.description: The veterinarian (and owner) must closely monitor the cat for signs of laryngeal edema (swelling of the throat) which can obstruct breathing. In rare, severe cases, oxygen therapy or securing an airway may be necessary.
treatments.name: Respiratory Monitoring / Airway Support
treatments.notes: While most cases result only in oral pain, significant swelling of the tongue and pharynx can lead to dyspnea (difficulty breathing). Immediate veterinary care is required if the cat shows signs of respiratory distress.Symptomatic Supportive Care

treatments.description: If the cat is unable to eat or drink due to oral pain, subcutaneous or intravenous fluids are administered to prevent dehydration. Analgesics (pain relief) may be prescribed to manage oral discomfort.
treatments.name: Fluid Therapy and Analgesics
treatments.notes: Most clinical signs of Anthurium ingestion subside over time without aggressive intervention. However, supportive care is vital if the cat refuses water due to mouth pain or experiences persistent vomiting.
